source_topic: How DNS works (turning a domain name into an IP address)

scenes.narration: Before your browser loads anything, it solves a puzzle. You type a name — but computers need numbers.

props.title: HOW DNS
props.subtitle: the 4-step handshake hiding behind every URL

scenes.narration: Every domain name must be translated into an IP address. Your browser cannot skip this step.

props.text: google.com means nothing to a server. It needs a number.

scenes.narration: Your browser asks a resolver. That resolver asks one of 13 root servers on Earth. The root points to the TLD server, the TLD to the authoritative server, which returns the IP.
That four-hop chain completes in just 20 milliseconds. But when DNS fails, everything fails. A DNS outage this June knocked out banking apps and government services for hours.

props.label: to resolve any domain on Earth — every single time

scenes.narration: The system is hierarchical. Root servers at the top, TLD servers below for .com and .org, then the authoritative nameserver — the one with your site's IP.
Caching makes it fast. Your OS and resolver remember recent lookups. A cached answer skips all four hops — under one millisecond.
DNS is invisible until it breaks — then nothing loads. Follow for one concept like this every day.

caption:
Your browser can't find google.com on its own — every time you visit, it asks a chain of 4 servers in the right order before anything loads 🔗

A DNS failure this June took down banking apps and government services for hours. One broken file. Entire internet sections gone.

The full 4-hop breakdown is in the reel 👆

Save this — DNS is one of those things every engineer should understand cold.

Follow @yourhandle for one concept a day.
